author: @VeniceInventors

author: @zenile7087
text:
Hi from France dear Rob,

Many thanks for this kind and poetic remembering of England's "Savoir-Faire" in Movements transformation.

Beyond the English Channel, at Calais, there is the Musée National de la Dentelle de Calais.

Giving example of traditional laces, some of them are 500+ years old, kept between glass tiles.

They have also genuine England-made mechanical broidery machines, made of cast iron, given by ...burglars, they were completeley knock-down in Dover falaise's caves, then after sailing these 3 tons+ machine were mounted. Visible at 135 Quai du Commerce, Calais, FR 62100; with 2 demonstration of producing lace, with reinforced ways of knitting for better durability.

People are rocking on wooden boards when knitting, the turning mass is very impressive ^^

Giving 10,000 single cotton strands wires from spools at the right place demands 3 entire months for a dedicated and highly skilled team of 2 technician, working 8 entire hours, 5 days a week.

These machines are using a perforated cardboard program; enhanced by Jacques de Vaucanson's life of mechanical construction, Monsieur Jacquard has taken all the merits of minor improvements. Nowadays it's a computer with electromechanical setup that is pushing/pulling a bundle of steel bars.

Tell us if you think to visit France in the future !

Best regards and Merry Christmas to everyone.
